CPT visited Beit Enoon School in Hebron, where education continues to face significant challenges.
Situated near a highway active with Israeli settlers, the school endures constant disruptions. The main route to the school is
Ministry of Education-the school remains a beacon of hope for 340 brave girls eager to learn.
After being closed for nearly a year due to safety concerns, the school reopened just last month, thanks to the persistent advocacy of families who want in-person education for their children. However, the closure resulted in 60 students transterring to other schools. The school was closed after October 7th and reopened only on September 2nd. The journey to school is challenging, with students and teachers often navigating treacherous routes, especially when Israeli soldiers implement checkpoints. The community has urgently called for protective measures to ensure safety as they reclaim their right to education.
… Deiaa Abu Al Jabeen is one of many heroes in the besieged Jabalia camp, we hope he and his family are okay as we have no information about his condition after the latest massacres in the camp.

At the entrance of Jabalia camp's market, the 32 years old Deiaa Abu Al Jabeen stands there making Palestinian Falafel with love. "Before the war, I used to work at an ice cream shop; Abu Zaitoun -It's known as the most famous ice cream shop in the North of Gaza-" Abu Al Jabeen continues,
"But when the war started, life suddenly stopped. I had to go and sell Falatel. There's nothing one can do, we want to provide for ourselves and our families." This is what he has expressed with gratitude.
Abu Al Jabeen spends his entire day hardworking so he can gain some Shekels, just like many other people in the camp who have lost their daily jobs and were obliged to work anything to provide the simplest essentials in the midst of the difficult circumstances people are living especially in the North, with the lack of resources and high prices….

The child, Joseph, now lives in a displacement tent at Al-Yarmouk Stadium, after being forcibly displaced from the Jabalia camp by the occupation. He turns to gymnastics to escape the crushing weight on his soul, as it has become his only refuge from the overwhelming pain.
With every leap, he finds a momentary release, restoring a fragile balance in the midst of destruction. For Joseph, gymnastics is not just a game-it's his sole means of healing his psyche, worn down by the horrors of war. Though the stadium is now his shelter, he longs for it to return to a place of joy and play, far from the fear that has invaded every part of his life.

For the first time in 30 years, Elbit's drone engine manufacturer made a significant loss.
This follows years of sustained direct action by Palestine Action and community mobilisation against the Israeli weapons maker.
Security costs rose significantly at the factory, contributing to increased expenses by £1million — and they still can't keep actionists away.
Turnover significantly decreased signifying less contracts and lower production, due to sustained disruption of the factory.
From a £2.5million operating profit in 2019 before we launched, UAV Engines had a £460K loss by the end of 2023.
When Elbit's factory in Tamworth faced a similar dire financial status due to reduced profits and increased security costs, they were forced to sell the factory.
The Tamworth site went from making parts for Israeli tanks to parts for public transport.
Direct action gets the goods.

Hello, world that enjoys peace, how do you feel surrounded by the warmth of your homes, sleeping in the embrace of safety? Do you realize how the life you lead is a distant dream for us? Here, in northern Gaza, we witness death every day, and children flee from the cruelty of war, searching for a semblance of safety in a world filled with tragedies. Look at this image; it tells the story of displaced people from the Jabalia camp, sleeping on the street with no shelter, their eyes carrying both sorrow and hope simultaneously, yearning for a tomorrow that might be better.
Is this the life you envisioned for your children?

These children were trapped in the Jabalia camp just a few days ago, and they emerged running under a barrage of death. Their innocent laughter still lights up their faces, as if their hearts are oblivious to the magnitude of the tragedy they are enduring. How much innocence resides within them, and how much sorrow is unworthy of their young ages? They insist on staying in their land in northern Gaza, refusing to be displaced to the south, as if the land itself is the safety they cling to. One of the children concludes with a simple wish: "I just want to live in safety." But where are the rights of childhood in all this devastation?

All summer, I was waiting to go back to school in the village of Zanouta near Hebron and start fifth grade with my friends.
We'd laugh and talk about our vacation and play together in the schoolyard. But when I arrived at the village with my father, I didn't find the school as I left it. I found it demolished, full of rubble and destruction. My heart felt broken with sadness.
I saw the books and toys we used to have, they were burned and destroyed. I keep asking myself why did this happen?
Why did the settlers attack our school? They don't want us to continue our education or live in peace.
I remembered my friends and how we used to play in the schoolyard after classes. Now the yard is empty, demolished and sad. I sat next to the rubble, and my tears were falling on my face. My school in Zanouta, was full of life and joy, now it has turned to piles of rubbles with nothing left but memories and unanswered questions.
Jana Abdullah Khudairat, 11 years

This child, Rashid, was buried underground with two floors above him, leaving no room for him to breathe. He was crying, trying to make me hear his voice, and he didn't calm down until I told him to stop crying and that I could hear him.
Rashid reminds me of our situation this year, as we are dying, crying, and screaming at the top of our lungs, but no one hears us or answers us.
But I heard Rashid and pulled him out from under the rubble after three hours of work, rescuing him after he had struggled for breath beneath two entire floors on his back. For the sake of Rashid and innocent children like him, I didn't stop my work; I didn't stop listening to their voices.

What is happening in North Gaza?
North Gaza, particularly Jabalia, has been enduring relentless bombardment for the past four days, with airstrikes, artillery, and ground invasions trapping civilians in a complete siege.
Over 250,000 residents are being pressured to evacuate to the south but they, along with journalists and aid workers, are facing sniper fire, bombings, and drone attacks once they attempt to move. Aid has been blocked for over two weeks, creating critical shortages of food, water, and medicine. The last operating bakery was also destroyed just last night.
Hospitals, including Kamal Adwan, Indonesian Hospital, and Al-Yaman as-Saeid Hospital, are surrounded, with Kamal Adwan ordered to evacuate despite the critical needs of injured and ICU patients and the lack of essential resources.

Journalist Mohammad al-Tanani was martyred, and his colleague Tamer Lobad was injured. Meanwhile, journalist Fadi al-Whidi sustained critical injuries after being shot in the neck by a sniper while covering events in Jabalia. Israeli forces are blocking ambulances from reaching the injured and martyred as the intense assault on North Gaza enters its fourth day.

Two days ago, I was in the Jabalia camp, which is currently being stormed by the Israeli occupation army. On the occasion of October 7th, we recall that Israeli aggression against Palestine and its people is not a one-time event but the continuation of a long series of systematic violations and crimes that began in 1948 and persist to this day. From the Nakba to daily bombings, killings, and forced displacements, Palestinians have endured decades of suffering.
Yet, after all these years, the Palestinian people continue to resist for their rights and freedom. Despite family displacements, home demolitions, arrests, and daily attacks, Palestinians remain steadfast in the face of these crimes. On this anniversary, we reaffirm that these crimes will not fade with time, and the Palestinian memory remains alive and unbroken.

A rehabilitation center for the disabled in Jabalia camp was bombed by the Israeli occupation forces a short while ago. Fares Afaneh, Director of Ambulance and Emergency Services in North Gaza, reported that several appeals were received from the location, prompting his emergency response team to rush to the scene. Upon their arrival, they were met with gunfire from Israeli forces stationed just 30 meters away. Many of the injured and martyrs from the attack are women and individuals with disabilities, specifically those who are deaf and mute. Afaneh urgently appeals to the international community and organizations like the Red Cross to allow ambulances to safely evacuate the injured, which include women, children, and the elderly, before more lives are lost.

OPINION: The ongoing mass bombardment and displacement in Jabalia and other parts of North Gaza is eerily reminiscent of the "Generals' Plan" proposed in early September by retired Israeli generals, such as Giora Eiland. This plan calls for imposing a complete siege on Northern Gaza, leaving civilians with no choice but to flee through corridors or face dire humanitarian consequences.
The plan also aims to starve out civilians by preventing the delivery of essential supplies, framing "humanitarian aid" as a strategic tool to coerce Hamas into submission.
Now, with airstrikes intensifying and thousands displaced, it appears the brutal measures outlined in this plan are being put into action under total media blackout. These actions clearly violate international humanitarian law, as the collective punishment of civilians-including starvation and cutting off medical aid-is both inhumane and illegal under international standards.

"Alon and Lebanon" is a children's book created for Israeli settler children, promoting the normalization of Jewish settlement in southern Lebanon. The book was written and crowd-funded by Prof. Amos Azaria, a member of the far-right "Uri Tzafon" (Wake Up North) movement, which includes figures like settler leader Daniella Weiss. The movement openly calls for the occupation and settlement of southern Lebanon, pushing their messianic agenda. The book, sold for 88 shekels (about $24) with an additional delivery fee, features generic Al-generated illustrations. For a higher price of 1,980 shekels, the author offers workshops at the buyer's home. Meanwhile, the Nachala movement, another Israeli settler organization activ in expanding illegal settlements in the West Bank, is advocating for the same occupation efforts in Lebanon.

Sayed Fadi is a Palestinian boy from Gaza.
His parents were miraculously able to get him out in the middle of the war, in hopes of affording him the necessary treatment. He's committed no crime. He just wants to live his life like all other children do without this excruciating pain.
Sayed is a victim of this bloody war and he has not had an opportunity to be treated even till now.
He suffers from dangerous spasms which causes him numerous problems with cerebral palsy.
Are the Arab and Western countries, and the doctors of the world, not able to treat this child or offer him help to be transferred to a specialist care center?
This is a call to our brothers in Jordan, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates, and any neurosurgery specialists, to take this opportunity to offer what is necessary in order to save this child.

On my way to the sea, I saw a father capturing moments of his children laughing, as if they were scattering joy across the waves. Their laughter stirred something in me during a time when Gaza has lost so much of its brightness. When I approached him, he told me that Muhammad and Bashar had emerged from the rubble just two days ago, and he brought them to the sea to envelop them in love and uplift their spirits. This is how we raise our children in Gaza; we teach them that love is our weapon against cruelty, and that family is our refuge in times of war.

‘Gaza habibti’ photo exhibition a heartwarming exhibition by Yura and Untold Palestine, in a collaboration Join us for the opening on Sunday, october 7, from 19:00 to 22:00. Meet our team, engage in discussions, and enjoy a screening of our short documentary.
Since 2019, a group of photographers has dedicated itself to collecting visual narratives from Palestine.
The driving force behind our endeavours has been the element of hope. With the current war in Gaza, everything has taken a dramatic turn.
We are now compelled to document stories of those who have been killed, yet carried the torch of hope.
Between the stories we recorded before the war and those we are capturing now, one clear narrative emerges: nothing can strip the Palestinian people of their humanity and their right to dream of freedom.
Opening Date: oct 7, 19:00 to 22:00
Venue: Darb 15 'Beit Arafa', street 15 off 87, building 11,
Maadi Cairo, Egypt
The exhibition will run until october 9.

I captured this image of my friend's mother, whose home was destroyed and replaced by a tent on the rubble. As she sits there, she reflects on the dignity she once had before the war and contemplates the devastation surrounding her. Despite losing her home and her youngest son, Muhammad, she transforms the destruction into love by planting green trees, bringing color to the ruins.
This embodies the belief that "life is narrow unless there's a glimpse of hope."
She dreamed of seeing her mother's face for almost ten months, her greatest wish was to see her, even through a TV screen... but she didn't see her... not even there!

We had no choice but to flee. We left our homes and sheep behind and moved to Wadi Khalil, where we lived in tents for 300 days. We carried the keys to our homes, clinging to the hope of returning one day. Finally, on August 21, 2024, a decision was made allowing us to return to our village. Celine Julani, the daughter of Ibrahim Julani, expresses the heartbreak of watching her family home in Jerusalem being demolished by their own hands. Following the occupation's municipal order to demolish their home due to "lack of a building permit," Celine explains how her brother has had to sleep at her grandfather's house for the past few days because they no longer have space for him. The part of the house they were forced to demolish was 80 square meters, built over 15 years ago. Celine, who had hoped to pursue her dreams and studies in that very room, now helps her father tear it down.

A Palestinian family in Gaza, displaced by the war, is now forced to live in their bombed house. The family, including seven members, used to live a normal life before the war turned them into refugees in their own home.
They now share a living room and kitchen, while many others in the neighborhood live in similar conditions or have set up tents on top of the ruins of their houses.

We Are Not Numbers: Julia Ramadan was martyred in an Israeli airstrike that targeted a residential building in the suburb of Ein al-Dilib, Saida. The strike claimed the lives of 45 people, including Julia and her mother, and injured 70 others. Before her tragic death, Julia had been actively helping others, as seen in her final social media post, where she called for assistance for a displaced family in Saida. Her death marked the end of a life committed to supporting her community. With rubble removal still underway, the martyred figure is expected to increase within the next few days.

"Gaza Habibti 
Photo Exhibition an heartwarming exhibition by
Yura and Untold Palestine, in a collaboration Join us for the opening on Sunday, october 7, from 19:00 to 22:00. Meet our team, engage in discussions, and enjoy a screening of our short documentary.
Since 2019, a group of photographers has dedicated itself to collecting visual narratives from Palestine.
The driving force behind our endeavours has been the element of hope. With the current war in Gaza, everything has taken a dramatic turn.
We are now compelled to document stories of those who have been killed, yet carried the torch of hope.
Between the stories we recorded before the war and those we are capturing now, one clear narrative emerges: nothing can strip the Palestinian people of their humanity and their right to dream of freedom.
Opening Date: oct 7, 19:00 to 22:00
Venue: Darb 15 'Beit Arafa', street 15 off 87, building 11,
Maadi Cairo, Egypt
The exhibition will run until october 9.
Made by Israel.

I saw Kanan Al-Khatib, a ten-year-old boy, whose home was destroyed in the recent attack. Parts of his body were burned, and his leg was injured. He looked with deep sorrow at his displaced friends playing football carefree as if his eyes reflected his profound sadness. How does a child like him feel, trapped by his disability, while longing to kick a ball with his foot? War steals from children their most basic rights to play, planting in their hearts the pain of loss and sorrow.

Thinking about the possibility of Israeli authorities accessing the addresses and numbers of Palestinian users is alarming, especially with the escalation of Israeli authorities in detaining Palestinians based on their posts on social media platforms during the genocide they commit in the Gaza Strip.
This is particularly highlighted by a survey by Sada Social Center during the early months of the genocide, which included 500 Palestinians, showed that 97% of the respondents reported owning or creating accounts on Telegram after the war to follow news platforms. This comes after 76% of them felt that social media platforms posed a threat to their lives and rights. Additionally, 84% of the sample reported not feeling safe while using private messaging apps, and 47% mentioned that they took steps to change their accounts, adjust their security settings, or resorted to using fake names.
Sada Social calls on fellow human rights organizations, both local and international, to intensify efforts in addressing these concerns and to exert the necessary pressure to prevent Telegram from officially slipping into the category of platforms that violate Palestinian digital rights, especially at a time when all things Palestinian are being increasingly restricted in the digital world.
There is a need to protect the privacy and data of Palestinian users.

Sada Social Center is closely following with great concern the announcement by Telegram platform's
CEO, Pavel Durov, that the platform will hand over the IP addresses and phone numbers of users to authorities and governments that have search warrants or other valid legal requests, in an effort to reduce crime.
Although Sada Social recognizes the importance of reducing crime and advocating for it on social media platforms, and acknowledges the need for genuine measures to achieve this, the center is deeply concerned that this measure might be used to target Palestinians and their content by Israel, violating their privacy. This concern is based on past instances where governments successfully pressured Telegram to block access to Palestinian channels through applications downloaded from the Apple and Google stores…

In the Gaza Strip, the water crisis has escalated into a humanitarian catastrophe due to the Israeli occupation, continuous massacres, destruction, and siege.
According to a July 2024 Oxfam report titled "Water War Crimes", the availability of clean water has plummeted by 94%, with residents receiving only 4.74 liters per person daily
—far below the WHO's emergency minimum of 15 liters per day and the non-emergency recommendation of 50-100 liters per day. This decline is a result of the systematic destruction of water infrastructure, including 88% of Gaza City's wells and 99% of its desalination plants (excluding smaller private plants), alongside fuel and spare parts restrictions. Some Gazans are receiving as little as 2 liters per day for all uses.

The little girl kept repeating in a voice filled with a mixture of sadness and hope: "I wish my hair would grow back before my birthday, so I can comb it beautifully and make two wonderful braids." This simple hope was her lifeline, a small dream in a sea of pain.
On a dark night in Rafah, little Sama Tabeel and her family were displaced in a tent when the sound of tanks pierced the silence, and panic spread. The occupation army suddenly entered, and the young girl's heart trembled with fear. She and her family rushed out of the tent in the middle of the night under a sky full of stars, witnesses to their tragedy.
A few days later, Sama began to notice strands of her hair falling out. With the recurring fear and anxiety, her hair gradually thinned. Every morning, she would find strands on her pillow, and the pain of loss went beyond her hair, growing within her with every painful memory she relived from that terrifying night.

Do you feel the pain of a child who was deprived of his childhood? Do you feel the pain of a crippled man who lost his family? Do you feel the pain of a young man whose dreams were crushed and whose future became hazy and was limited to collecting firewood and filling water? Do you feel the pain of a grandmother who stood to see where her house was in this destruction so she could go and recite Al-Fatiha for her grandchildren who died under the rubble? Maha, a five-year-old girl, lost her mother, father, and siblings in a brutal airstrike that destroyed their home. She was thrown from the rubble as flames engulfed her small body, and on a rainy night, she crawled toward a tree, where a dog approached her. She mistook the dog for her mother. With a burned face and damaged nerves in her hands and legs, Maha endures the physical and psychological scars of war. Fear haunts her eyes, and pain weighs heavily on her little soul. All she wishes for is for the war to end, so she can return to the embrace of the family she lost.

"I'm looking at the school where I
was supposed to be playing with my friends right now. But the war has forced me to live here, after my home was destroyed and my friends were martyred. My soccer ball is torn, but I still love studying. My bag, which once carried my books, now carries the weight of constant displacement. We, the children of Gaza, live in a state of loss. All I dream of is something simple: to play and study. Is that too much to ask?
Is that a crime we should be punished for?"

Today, the schools handed out ID cards for teachers and students so they can identify themselves at checkpoints as belonging to the school. There were no incidents, and everyone was allowed to pass through.

The children of Gaza, deprived of the simplest rights of their childhood, now face another year amidst the flames of war and the echoes of destruction. They live in schools that were meant to be places of learning and spaces where their dreams would take shape, yet they have become shelters that cover their wounds and bear witness to their harsh reality. Their schools no longer teach them letters but have become silent observers of life's cruelty. Yet, despite it all, their eyes still carry remnants of dreams that refuse to fade, and a childhood that continues to cry out for a better life.

Suwar Abu Housa and Jouwan Abu Housa are at risk of going completely blind if they do not receive urgent surgeries. Suwar has severe vision impairments, eye inflammation, cloudy vision, and fluid buildup, and her condition is rapidly deteriorating. Jouwan also underwent eye surgery before the war, but her condition worsened after it began. Both girls need immediate medical intervention abroad, as there is no available treatment in Gaza.

One shot, and a long silence...
A single shot from a drone pierced the face of the little girl, Burooj Mahfouz. That shot was enough to silence her from the very first moment and prevent her from eating regular food. Now, she only consumes mashed food and liquids. She can no longer speak like she used to and now communicates through sign language, after once being able to speak with everyone normally.
Burooj used to play with other children, learn, eat, drink, and live her life just like any other child, until her life was suddenly turned upside down.

I previously captured a photo of a little girl crying bitterly because she didn't receive a slice of pizza during a pizza distribution event for children.
After the photo gained significant attention and interaction from people, I returned to the same location to find the girl,
"Rosalina." I found her and got to know her family, consisting of 12 members, and witnessed the harsh conditions they live
Thanks to a kind initiative by a professional chef, we made pizza, and the girl helped us with some of the preparation.
Her emotions were a mix of deep shyness and happiness.
She wondered why she was receiving this unusual attention, given the difficult circumstances surrounding her. She also felt great joy in eating a clean, delicious pizza-something she rarely experiences in such challenging conditions.
This little girl is just one example of the thousands of children living in the toughest of circumstances.

Under the current siege, the flow of essential goods is also severely restricted. Trucks carrying food and other supplies are barred from entering Hebron, creating a dire shortage of necessities. The city is essentially under lockdown, with no one allowed to exit to other cities or even approach the checkpoints that now serve as barriers to freedom. Residents quickly seize the opportunity when soldiers are not near the closed gates to exchange food supplies, transferring them from one vehicle to another in a hurried attempt to sustain themselves.
In addition to these repressive tactics, the occupation forces are conducting widespread arrests in Hebron and its surrounding villages, The policy of demolishing homes continues unabated, creating a climate of fear.
Simultaneously, settler violence against Palestinians escalates daily, often occurring under the protection of the occupation army. This atmosphere of violence and repression has become a constant reality for the residents of Hebron, who face increasing hardship and insecurity with each passing day.

The Israeli occupation repeatedly uses collective punishment on the civilians of Al-Khalil/Hebron, imposing a comprehensive siege on the city and its surrounding villages. Since August 31, they have closed all roads and entrances leading to the city, effectively cutting it off from nearby villages. Cement blocks with iron gates are strategically placed on these roads, even blocking off dirt paths that residents sometimes use as alternative routes.
Strict prohibitions on movement near these closed roads and iron gates makes the situation dire; soldiers use force to deter movement and maintain the blockade: they beat pedestrians, or shoot at vehicles or individuals approaching the barriers.
For residents living in the areas under strict lockdown, living conditions are harsh. For some, they are unable to move freely or return to their homes once they leave...

Among the ruins of our destroyed homes in the Shati retugee camp, Gaza City, on the walls that have become silent witnesses to injustice, we engraved our sorrows and hopes. On one of them, they wrote "By U.S.A," a mark of oppression that will never fade, but it won't extinguish the faith in our hearts that "Indeed, with hardship comes ease." In the corners of the rubble, echoes of longing for our loved ones in the south resound, as if every stone here cries out: We miss you. On another wall, we inscribed with our tears and sweat: "We will rebuild, no matter what," a testament to our determination to restore Gaza despite Israel's oppression. We are from Gaza, rising from the heart of our wounds, emerging from the ashes, and we will never forget that we stand firm until dawn returns.

The story of Ahmed Al-Juaidi documents the crime of ethnic cleansing carried out by the Israeli occupation army against Palestinians in Gaza. He witnessed a video published by occupation soldiers on their personal accounts showing Israeli army bulldozers demolishing his family's home in the Tel Al-Sultan neighborhood, west of Rafah. The house consists of four floors and houses seven families.

The ongoing polio vaccination campaign in Gaza, which aims to inoculate 640,000 children under the age of 10, showcases the hypocrisy in the current geopolitical landscape. Despite the urgent need for vaccinations, there is no comprehensive truce to ensure these children's safety, only temporary
"humanitarian pauses" in specific places that Israel has agreed to amid its continuous military operations in the Strip. These pauses are far from sufficient, given the continuous threat of violence from the occupation forces even within safe zones, and reflect a deep irony: the same global powers funding the World Health Organization (WHO) to administer these life-saving vaccines are also supplying the weapons that endanger these children's lives daily.

Amidst the rubble of war and the constant sound of planes in the sky, the children of Gaza find a space to play soccer in the destroyed streets, unfazed by the looming danger. These spontaneous moments echo Macklemore's words, questioning the fear of seeking peace and calling for the freedom of Palestine.
The video tells the story of children who defy their harsh reality and confront occupation with their innocence and resilience, delivering a powerful message of strength, survival, and the unwavering grip on life.

Where is Duha Talat? The 8-year-old girl was reportedly taken by the Israeli military near Rafah crossing in late July 2024. A now-deleted Instagram post by Ido Zahar, a soldier from Israel's 432nd Tzabar Battalion, showed him with a girl in Gaza, believed to be Duha. A soldier, anonymized as "I," recounted that during a raid near the Rafah crossing, an elderly man came out from a house with an 8 year old firl and offered to reveal tunnel shafts, which turned out to be sewer shafts. Accused by Shin Bet of H*mas ties, the man was detained and taken.
Duha, separated from her only family, was handed over to other soldiers; her current whereabouts still remain unknown. Reports indicate hundreds of children are missing in Gaza, raising further concerns about their fate ir the hands of occupation soldiers.

Thanks to the unwavering support of our friends in South Africa and the dedicated PCRF Treatment Abroad team, Mira will receive urgent medical care for a complex fracture in her right leg and a skin graft due to injuries from Israeli bombing attacks.
This partnership is a powerful reminder that together, we can bring hope and healing across borders.
Many more children from Gaza urgently need evacuation and medical treatment. Your continued support is crucial to their recovery.

Zakariya Al-Adra is a Palestinian man from Al-Tawani, a village in the Khaleel area of the West Bank, Palestine, who was shot and severely wounded by an Israeli settler on October 13th, 2023. The incident took place during a violent attack by settlers from the Havat Ma'on settlement, who shot Zakariya in the stomach at point-blank range. The shooting left him with life-altering injuries, including damage to his stomach, intestines, pancreas, and ribs. He has since undergone several surgeries and continues to suffer from chronic pain. The attack is part of a broader pattern of escalating settler violence in the West Bank, particularly targeting Palestinian communities in areas like Masafer Yatta, which are under constant threat of forced eviction by Israeli authorities.

325 days of genocide and counting.

Their hair has turned grey, and the weight of their worries has become almost unbearable, pressing heavily on their hearts. They toil under the sun, risking their lives near the Israeli army's danger zones, just to gather firewood, hoping only to feed their families and stave off their hunger. For the past eleven months, gas and every basic necessity have been cut off from northern Gaza, forcing them into a primitive existence akin to that of cavemen. Yet, the men of Gaza still possess tender hearts and souls that cherish life, though these hearts are now weary from sorrow. Here, the cries of men echo with the anguish of a father who has lost his child and the grief of a husband who has become a widower. All their efforts and years of struggle seem wasted, but despite everything, their spirits remain unbroken, still fighting to survive.

The health sector in Gaza is facing severe shortages due to the ongoing war, with a critical shortage of water exacerbating the situation. This lack of water has led to widespread hygiene issues, including the spread of lice, nits, and other diseases among children, particularly those living in tents. The scarcity of basic cleaning supplies, such as shampoo and soap, has made it nearly impossible for families to maintain cleanliness, leading to a deteriorating public health situation. The spread of these conditions is particularly troubling in a population already vulnerable due to displacement and inadequate medical resources.

‏Despite this devastating tragedy, Diaa did not lose hope or give up. He remained determined to keep moving forward and fulfill his dreams, hoping to one day obtain prosthetic limbs that would allow him to return to his beloved hobby of photography.

‏Diaa’s story is one of thousands of stories in Gaza. According to the Palestinian Ministry of Health, over 10,000 children in Gaza lost their limbs during the genocide

Diaa Al-Odaini is a 15-year-old boy living in Deir Al-Balah, Gaza. Before the war broke out, Diaa had a dream of becoming a professional photographer. He would spend hours capturing different scenes and events, patiently saving up to buy a professional camera that would allow him to develop his skills and achieve his ambitions.

‏Diaa was saving his pocket money to buy the camera he had always dreamed of. In fact, he had managed to enroll in a photography course just a month before the war started, which had only intensified his passion and love for photography.

‏However, the harsh realities of the war in Gaza soon caught up with Diaa. One day, while he was sitting with his friends playing video games, a rocket struck the building, causing Diaa to lose both his arms. In that moment, his life was forever changed….

Every time the occupation army storms the Nur Shams refugee camp in the city of Tulkarm in the northern West Bank, it leaves behind a number of martyrs, some of whom die because the occupation army prevents ambulances from reaching and treating the wounded, in addition to causing significant destruction to the infrastructure, homes, and properties of citizens.

In Umm Al-Khair, Eid Al-Hathaleen reports on the horrific events that took place on July 26, and August 14, 2024. On these days, the Israeli military and border police entered their village with bulldozers and forced the residents to pack up their belongings and leave within 30 minutes. The villagers barely had time to get their personal belongings out of their houses before they saw their homes destroyed by the bulldozers. "We live in fear every day that the settlers will come and burn down our entire village," reports Al-Hathaleen.
The villagers feel dehumanized and not seen as human beings by the Israeli army and the settlers. It is important that the events in the West Bank continue to be reported, as the sneaky annexation by the Israeli government and the ongoing discrimination against Palestinians continues to go forward unhindered.
The world is so focused on the war in Gaza that events in the West Bank, including the increasing settler violence in the north and south, receive little attention.

…The genocide in Gaza is a stark and brutal testament to the inhumanity perpetuated by Zionist imperialist forces against a resilient population. The official death toll tells us that over 40,686 lives have been obliterated, with more than 16,456 of those being innocent children. With over 454 educational facilities being destroyed, this is not merely an infrastructural assault; it is a calculated effort to extinguish a people's existence and future.

As we enter the back-to-school season, the tragedy of Palestine unfolds with chilling clarity. Gaza's educational institutions, once bustling with the hopes and dreams of its youth, have been reduced to rubble. Yet, the destruction of schools is only a visible marker of a deeper catastrophe-the eradication of the very children who should fill those classrooms. These empty desks and shattered schools symbolize the erasure of an entire generation, silenced before they could even reach their full potential.
This genocidal aggression is a deliberate strategy to dismantle the fabric of Gaza's society, a grim reminder of colonialist tactics designed to crush resistance and foster despair. The martyred children and their destroyed schools is a stark indictment of an international system that stands complicit, prioritizing geopolitical interests over innocent human lives…..

Former Israeli Defense Minister and current Knesset member Lieberman is calling for depriving the Gaza Strip of water, electricity, fuel, and goods, which contradicts international humanitarian law that the occupying power is responsible for providing basic needs to the civilian population in the territories it occupies. This includes the provision of food, water, healthcare, and essential services such as electricity and water. This responsibility is enshrined in the Geneva Conventions, particularly the Fourth Convention, which concerns the protection of civilians during wartime. Moreover, the occupying power is prohibited from using collective punishment or depriving civilians of these essential services as a means of pressure or retaliation.

Ahmad Husam Hammad, a 5-year-old boy, urgently needs to be evacuated from Gaza to replace the battery in his pacemaker, which he has outgrown. Ahmad suffers from inconsistent heartbeats, developmental delays, lack of oxygen, and constant seizures, making his condition extremely serious. Doctors have given a critical deadline of 10 days for the procedure to be carried out, as the pacemaker is essential for regulating his heartbeats and preventing further complications. Sama Tbeil is a young Palestinian girl from Gaza who lost her hair due to the trauma she endured during the ongoing Israeli aggression. After being displaced with her family from Rafah to Khan Yunis, Sama started noticing her hair falling out, which she now attributes to the extreme stress and fear caused by the shelling and gunfire. Her hair loss has led to emotional distress, as she faces questions from other children about her appearance. Sama wishes for her hair to grow back and hopes to find proper medical treatment, but the ongoing blockade and lack of medical supplies in Gaza make this difficult.

Hamza Abu Halima, referred to as the "Lion of Gaza," became a symbol of resistance and resilience after a distressing image of him, taken during an Israeli forces' interrogation, went viral. The photograph showed him naked, handcuffed, and in front of an Israeli soldier, intended to break his spirit but instead just ended up underscoring his defiance. The ordeal took place after Israeli forces targeted his family's home, leading to the deaths of several relatives and his subsequent capture.
Despite enduring physical and emotional torment, Hamza's steadfast gaze in the face of such brutality has turned him into an important figure of Palestinian struggle, epitomizing the broader resilience of the people in Gaza against the savagery of the occupation forces.

Dr. Oday Alkatary, a pediatric dentist in Gaza, has initiated an innovative project known as the "Gaza Kids Dental Tent" to provide essential dental care to children in displacement camps. Due to the occupation forces presence in Gaza and the circumstances, including frequent displacement and lack of resources, Dr. Alkatary transformed a car seat into a makeshift dentist's chair. His mobile clinic, powered by a gas cylinder when electricity is unavailable, not only offers dental treatment but also raises awareness about dental hygiene among children and provides them with mental health support.

Inshirah Saeed Al-Madani, a displaced child of Al-Nuseirat camp in Gaza, is suffering from a severe case of cancer that has already claimed one of her eyes. She urgently needs to travel abroad for treatment to prevent the loss of her remaining eye, as no adequate medical care is available in Gaza. Inshirah's condition is worsening, with her eye frequently bleeding, and the tumor growing larger by the day. She expresses a deep desire to receive treatment abroad, like others who have managed to leave Gaza for life-saving care.
